title: Natural Gas Leak (Indoor Exposure)
keywords: I smell gas in my house
smell of rotten eggs in kitchen
steps: Do NOT touch any electrical switches, light bulbs, or appliances (even the doorbell).
Do NOT use phones or lighters inside the building.
Open all windows and doors immediately to ventilate.
Evacuate all occupants and pets immediately.
Call the gas utility company and emergency services from OUTSIDE the building.
Do not re-enter until officially cleared by the fire department or utility workers.

title: Natural Gas Leak (Outdoor/Neighbor)
keywords: I smell gas in the street
reporting gas leak for someone else
steps: Do NOT enter the area where the smell is strongest.
Warn others in the immediate vicinity to keep away.
Do NOT smoke or use any ignition sources near the suspected leak.
Call emergency services and provide the exact location or nearest cross-street.
If you suspect a neighbor is inside, knock loudly but do NOT use the doorbell.
Wait for emergency services at a safe distance (at least 100 meters).

title: Earthquake
steps: DROP to the ground before the earthquake drops you.
COVER your head and neck with your arms and seek shelter under a sturdy table.
HOLD ON to your shelter until the shaking stops.
If outdoors, move to an open area away from buildings, trees, and power lines.
If in a vehicle, stop as quickly as safety permits and stay inside.
Be prepared for aftershocks.

title: Flash Flood (Trapped/Submerged)
steps: Move to the highest level of the building. If in a car that stalls, exit immediately and move to higher ground.
If you cannot reach high ground, climb onto the roof. Do NOT climb into a closed attic.
Avoid contact with moving water; even shallow water can sweep you away.
Stay away from electrical appliances and the main power box.
Signal for help using a flashlight or bright cloth.
Do NOT attempt to swim through floodwaters unless absolutely necessary for survival.

title: Flash Flood (Roadway/Sighting)
keywords: I see a flood on the road
steps: Turn Around, Don't Drown! Do NOT attempt to drive or walk through the water.
Report the location of the flooding to local emergency services.
Warn other drivers and pedestrians to stay away from the area.
Move to high ground and monitor the situation from a safe distance.
Do NOT approach the water's edge; banks can be unstable and collapse.
If you see someone trapped, call 911 immediately. Do NOT attempt a water rescue yourself.

title: Chemical Spill (Direct Exposure)
keywords: my eyes are burning from a spill
steps: Move upwind and away from the source of the chemical immediately.
If the chemical is on your skin or clothes, remove contaminated clothing and flush skin with cool water for 15-20 mins.
If in eyes, flush with water continuously while holding eyelids open.
Do NOT use neutralizing agents (like vinegar or baking soda) on skin.
Seek immediate medical attention and tell them exactly what substance you were exposed to.
Seal contaminated clothing in a plastic bag to prevent further exposure.

title: Chemical Spill (Sighting/Reporting)
steps: Maintain a safe distance (at least 500 meters) and move upwind.
Call emergency services and report the location and type of spill.
If safe, identify the UN number or hazard label from a distance using binoculars.
Prevent others from entering the danger zone.
Do NOT touch, walk through, or inhale the substance.
Provide details on the size of the spill and if it is entering drains or waterways.

title: Landslide / Mudslide
steps: If you are inside, stay inside. Move to the part of the building furthest from the slope.
If you are outside, run to the nearest high ground away from the path of the slide.
If escape is impossible, curl into a tight ball and protect your head.
Listen for unusual sounds like trees cracking or boulders knocking together.
Stay away from the slide area; additional slides may occur.
Check for injured or trapped persons without entering the slide path directly.

title: Tornado / High Wind Shelter
steps: Go to a pre-designated 'safe room', basement, or the lowest level of the building.
Inner hallways and closets are the safest spots if no basement is available.
Stay away from windows, glass, and exterior walls.
Get under a sturdy piece of furniture (like a heavy table) and use your arms to protect your head and neck.
If outdoors or in a mobile home, seek shelter in a sturdy building immediately.
As a last resort, lie flat in a low-lying area (like a ditch) and cover your head.

title: Radiological Hazard (In Fallout Zone)
keywords: I am in the radiation zone
steps: GET INSIDE immediately. Use the center of the building or a basement.
STAY INSIDE. Close and seal all windows and doors. Turn off AC/Heating systems.
Remove your outer layer of clothing and seal it in a plastic bag to discard contamination.
Wash your skin and hair gently with soap and water; do NOT use hair conditioner.
Cover any open wounds to prevent radioactive particles from entering.
Do NOT eat or drink anything that was uncovered during the event.

title: Radiological Hazard (Incident Reporting)
steps: Seek shelter in the nearest sturdy building immediately.
Warn others to seek indoor shelter and avoid the direction of the plume.
Monitor official emergency broadcasts for evacuation routes or shelter-in-place orders.
Do NOT travel toward the incident site to help; you will become a victim.
Notify emergency services of the time and location of the initial sighting.
Stay calm and wait for further instructions from radiological experts.

title: Extreme Heat / Heatwave
steps: Stay in air-conditioned buildings as much as possible.
Drink more water than usual and avoid alcohol or high-sugar drinks.
Wear lightweight, light-colored, and loose-fitting clothing.
Limit outdoor activity, especially during the middle of the day.
Never leave children or pets in a parked car, even with windows cracked.
Check on neighbors, especially the elderly or those with chronic conditions.

title: Extreme Cold / Winter Storm
steps: Stay indoors and dress in warm layers.
If you must go outside, cover all exposed skin to prevent frostbite.
Keep the heat at a consistent temperature to prevent pipes from freezing.
Avoid traveling. If stranded in a car, stay inside and run the engine only 10 minutes every hour for heat.
Ensure your emergency kit has extra blankets and high-energy food.
Watch for signs of hypothermia: shivering, exhaustion, confusion, or fumbling hands.

title: Thunderstorm / Lightning
steps: When thunder roars, go indoors. Seek shelter in a substantial building or hard-topped vehicle.
Avoid using corded phones, computers, or other electrical equipment that puts you in contact with electricity.
Stay away from plumbing, including sinks, baths, and faucets.
If caught outdoors with no shelter, move away from tall objects like trees and poles.
Do NOT lie flat on the ground. Squat low on the balls of your feet to minimize ground contact.
Wait at least 30 minutes after the last clap of thunder before heading back outside.

title: Sandstorm (In Zero Visibility)
keywords: I am driving in a sandstorm
I can't see anything on the road
steps: Pull off the road as far as possible. Stop and turn OFF all vehicle lights.
Take your foot off the brake. You want your car to be invisible to drivers behind you.
Stay inside your vehicle with windows and vents tightly closed.
Cover your face with a damp cloth if dust is entering the cabin.
Wait until visibility improves before attempting to drive again.
If on foot, seek shelter behind a solid structure and protect your eyes and lungs.

title: Sandstorm (Wall of Dust Sighting)
keywords: I see a giant wall of dust
sky is turning orange with dust
steps: Seek shelter in a sturdy building immediately.
Close all windows, doors, and air intake vents.
Bring pets and livestock into protected areas.
Warn others who may be outdoors or driving in the path of the storm.
Avoid traveling if you see a dust storm approaching.
Ensure you have enough water and lighting in case of power or utility failure.

title: Volcanic Eruption (In Danger Zone)
keywords: I am near a volcano eruption
steps: EVACUATE IMMEDIATELY if in a designated danger zone. Do not wait.
PROTECT LUNGS: Wear an N95 mask or a damp cloth over your nose and mouth.
STAY INDOORS if not evacuating. Close windows and seal vents with tape.
STAY CLEAR of valleys and low-lying areas (lahars/mudslides follow these paths).
WEAR goggles and long clothing to protect from abrasive ash.
DO NOT DRIVE in heavy ash; it will stall your engine and visibility is zero.

title: Volcanic Eruption (Distance Witness)
keywords: reporting ash cloud in the distance
steps: STAY AWAY from the volcano. Do not travel toward it to take photos.
MONITOR local authorities for evacuation updates and ashfall advisories.
HELP neighbors who may be vulnerable to air quality changes.
PREPARE for potential flight cancellations or road closures in the region.
Keep a battery-powered radio for emergency updates if power fails.
Report the height and color of the ash plume to local geological services.

title: Tsunami (Immediate Impact/Coast)
keywords: I am trapped in a tsunami
steps: MOVE INLAND and to HIGH GROUND immediately. Do not wait for a siren.
VERTICAL EVACUATION: If you cannot reach high ground, climb to the 3rd floor or higher of a reinforced concrete building.
IF IN WATER: Grab onto something that floats (tree, door, roof) and hold on.
STAY AWAY: Do not go to the coast for at least 24 hours; more waves will come.
SIGNAL: Once on high ground, signal your location for rescue.
DO NOT enter your car if the wave is close; it will become a trap.

title: Tsunami (Warning/Reporting)
keywords: I felt a massive quake near the sea
steps: Warn anyone on the beach or in low-lying coastal areas to move inland immediately.
Call emergency services to report observations of receding water or unusual surges.
Move yourself to a safe elevation before the first wave arrives.
Monitor official tsunami warning center broadcasts for the 'all clear'.
Do NOT return to the coast to 'check' on property until cleared.
Alert local maritime traffic via radio if possible.

title: Sinkhole / Ground Collapse
steps: Evacuate the building or area immediately if you notice sudden large cracks or the ground sinking.
Stay away from the edges of the sinkhole; the ground may continue to collapse.
Cordon off the area with tape or cones to prevent others from falling in.
Do NOT attempt to fill the hole or investigate the bottom.
Check for utility leaks (gas, water, sewer) that may have been caused by the collapse.
Contact emergency services and a structural engineer to assess the safety of nearby structures.

title: Hazardous Air Quality
steps: Stay indoors as much as possible with windows and doors closed.
Use high-efficiency (HEPA) air purifiers if available.
Avoid strenuous outdoor activity or exercise until the air quality improves.
If you must go outside, wear a well-fitted N95 or P100 respirator mask.
Keep indoor air clean by avoiding smoking, vacuuming, or using candles.
Monitor air quality indices (AQI) regularly through official weather apps.

title: Avalanche
steps: IF CAUGHT: Try to stay on the surface by 'swimming' through the snow. Push toward the side of the slide.
AIR POCKET: Before the snow stops, cup your hands in front of your face to create an air pocket.
STAY CALM: Conserve oxygen. Do NOT shout unless you hear rescuers above you.
FOR WITNESSES: Mark the spot where you last saw the victim. Call for help immediately.
SEARCH: Use beacons, probes, and shovels if you are trained and equipped.
AVOID: Stay away from similar slopes; a second avalanche often follows.

title: Solar Flare / Geomagnetic Storm (EMP)
steps: Disconnect sensitive electronics from power outlets during extreme solar events.
Be prepared for prolonged power outages and communication failures (GPS, Internet).
Ensure you have a battery-powered or hand-crank radio for news.
Keep a paper map of your area in case GPS/Navigation systems fail.
Avoid using elevators if a geomagnetic storm alert is active.
Check backup generators and fuel supplies for critical infrastructure.

title: Hurricane / Tropical Cyclone
steps: Board up windows and secure all outdoor objects (furniture, trash cans).
Fill bathtubs and containers with clean water in case of utility failure.
Evacuate immediately if you are in a designated storm surge zone.
Do NOT go outside during the 'eye' of the storm; the winds will return from the opposite direction suddenly.
Stay away from windows and take shelter in an interior room on the lowest floor.
Turn off propane tanks and main power if flooding is imminent.

title: Dangerous Animals / Insects (Attack/Swarm)
keywords: I am being attacked by a dog
steps: If attacked by an animal, try to place an object (bag, jacket) between you and the animal.
If stung by bees, move to a safe indoor area immediately. Do NOT swat at them.
For snake bites: Keep the limb still and below heart level. Do NOT attempt to suck out venom.
Call emergency services and specify the type of animal or insect involved.
Remove yourself from the immediate danger zone without running if possible (running can trigger chase instinct).

title: Dangerous Animal / Insect (Sighting)
keywords: reporting a bear in the neighborhood
steps: Maintain a safe distance and do NOT approach the animal.
Warn others in the vicinity to keep away and bring pets indoors.
Report the location, type of animal, and its behavior to emergency services.
Monitor the animal from a safe, enclosed location (like a house or car).
Do NOT attempt to capture or feed the animal.

title: Elevator Entrapment (Victim / Inside)
keywords: I am stuck in the lift
steps: Stay calm. Press the 'Alarm' or 'Phone' button inside the elevator.
Do NOT attempt to pry the doors open or exit if it is between floors.
Call emergency services and provide the building address and elevator number if known.
Sit on the floor and wait for professional help. The elevator is safe and has enough air.

title: Utility Failure / Power Outage
steps: Check your circuit breakers to see if it is a local issue.
Unplug sensitive electronics to protect them from power surges when electricity returns.
Keep refrigerator and freezer doors closed to preserve food.
Use flashlights instead of candles to prevent fire hazards.
If you see downed power lines, stay at least 35 feet away and call emergency services.
Listen to a battery-powered radio for updates from the utility company.
